text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Arrow case labels (->) do not fall through. Only the matching branch executes, printing 'zero'.

text: Which of the following are valid switch expression result types? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Switch expressions can yield any non-void type. void is not a valid yield type — switch expressions must produce a value.

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: When j==1, continue outer skips to the next iteration of the outer loop. Only j==0 is ever printed per outer iteration, giving 00, 10, 20.

text: Which switch expression syntax correctly assigns a multi-line result?
explanation: In a switch expression block ({}), the yield statement provides the value. return is not used in switch expressions. Arrow case with a block uses yield, not return.

text: What is the output of the following code? (dangling else)
explanation: The dangling else rule in Java: an else always binds to the nearest preceding if. Here the else belongs to 'if (x > 10)', not 'if (x > 3)'. Since x=5 satisfies x>3 but not x>10, the else branch executes, printing 'small'.

text: What does the following ternary expression evaluate to?
explanation: a > b is false (2 > 3), so the false branch evaluates: b > 1 is true (3 > 1), so b * 3 = 9 is returned. The ternary operator is right-associative: a > b ? ... : (b > 1 ? b*3 : 0).

text: What is the output of the following traditional switch statement?
explanation: In a traditional switch statement, execution falls through each case until a break is encountered. Matching starts at case 2, then falls through to case 3 which has a break. Output: 'Tue Wed Done'.

text: Which of the following switch expressions compile without error? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: int n = switch(x) { case 1 -> { System.out.println(); yield 1; } default -> 0; };

explanation: A: valid arrow switch expression with default — compiles. B: valid block case with yield — compiles. C: a switch expression used as a statement is allowed if the switch type is void-compatible (all arms are void), but here there is no default, which is only required when the result is used; used as statement without default is actually allowed — however the question tests expression context. D: missing default in an expression context where all enum values are not covered — compiler error because exhaustiveness cannot be guaranteed for int.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The while loop checks the condition before each iteration. i starts at 0, prints 0, 1, 2 (while i < 3 is true), then when i becomes 3 the condition is false and the loop exits. Output: '0 1 2 end'.

text: What is the output of the following do-while code when the condition is initially false?
explanation: A do-while loop always executes its body at least once before evaluating the condition. count=10 is printed, then count becomes 11. The condition 11 < 5 is false, so the loop exits. Output: '10 done'.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: break outer terminates both loops when i==1 and j==1. The iterations that print before that break are: (0,0), (0,1), (0,2), (1,0). When (i=1, j=1), break outer fires and no more output follows.

text: Which of the following statements about the enhanced for loop are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: It can iterate over any object that implements java.lang.Iterable.
The loop variable is a reference to the original array element and can be used to modify the array.
It does not expose an index variable.
It can iterate over a Map directly.

explanation: A: Enhanced for works with any Iterable. B: It also works with arrays directly. D: There is no index variable — only the element value. C is incorrect: for primitive arrays the loop variable is a copy, so modifying it does not affect the original array; for object arrays you get a reference to the object but reassigning the variable does not change the array slot. E is incorrect: Map does not implement Iterable; you iterate over keySet(), values(), or entrySet().

text: Which of the following code snippets causes a compilation error?
explanation: In Java, the condition of an if statement must be a boolean expression. Option B uses assignment (x = 1) which has type int, not boolean — this is a compiler error. (Unlike C/C++, Java does not implicitly convert int to boolean.)

text: What happens when the following code is compiled?

options.text: Compiles and prints nothing at runtime.

explanation: Java's compiler performs flow analysis and detects unreachable statements. Since both branches of the if/else unconditionally return, the println after the if/else is unreachable. The compiler reports an 'unreachable statement' error and refuses to compile.

text: Which of the following correctly demonstrate the use of yield in a switch expression? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: int r = switch(x) { case 1 -> { int v = x * 2; yield v; } default -> 0; };
int r = switch(x) { case 1: yield x * 2; default: yield 0; };
int r = switch(x) { case 1 -> yield x * 2; default -> yield 0; };
int r = switch(x) { case 1: { yield x * 2; } default: yield 0; };

explanation: A: Arrow case with a block — yield is correct inside {}. B: Colon case labels always use yield (or fall-through + yield) to produce a value. D: A colon case can have a block with yield inside. C is invalid: 'case 1 -> yield x * 2' is a syntax error — arrow cases with a single expression do not use yield; yield is only valid inside a block ({}).
